skill.name: fetching-library-docs
skill.description: Fetches focused library documentation through Context7 MCP and filters the result into concise examples, API signatures, and notes. It is intended for published package documentation such as React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, and similar libraries.
skill.summary: Fetch focused Context7 library docs with concise examples, signatures, and notes.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified scripts, network access, filesystem access, and external command execution, but many Markdown backtick and weak-crypto alerts are false positives from documentation examples. The main confirmed risk is operational: the skill runs Context7 through npx and includes a generic MCP stdio client that executes command strings with shell=True.

medium_findings.title: Generic MCP stdio command execution
medium_findings.description: The MCP client accepts a --stdio command string and launches it with subprocess.Popen using shell=True. This is legitimate for MCP server startup, but it can execute arbitrary shell commands if a user or wrapper passes untrusted input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call directly uses shell=True with a command string supplied by the CLI. The skill normally passes a hardcoded Context7 command, so this is a confirmed risky capability rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Automatic execution of remote npm package
medium_findings.description: The main fetch path starts Context7 through npx -y @upstash/context7-mcp. This performs network-dependent package execution and should be treated as an external code execution dependency.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The npx command is explicit and is required by the skill workflow. The package name is fixed, which reduces command injection risk, but the dependency still executes downloaded external code.

low_findings.title: Unescaped JSON parameter construction
low_findings.description: Shell scripts interpolate library, topic, mode, and page values into JSON strings without JSON escaping. Malformed input can break requests or alter tool parameters, but no shell injection path was confirmed from this construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is visible and user-controlled values can affect JSON syntax. The resulting string is passed as a Python argument, so evidence supports robustness risk but not direct code execution.

low_findings.title: Predictable temporary PID files and process killing
low_findings.description: Server helper scripts write predictable PID files under /tmp and stop processes by PID or process-name pattern. This is common local tooling, but it can affect unintended local processes if misused.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The PID-file and kill behavior is explicit in the helper scripts. The scope appears local and user-initiated, so the security impact is limited.

low_findings.title: Static documentation pattern false positives
low_findings.description: Most Ruby backtick and weak-cryptography alerts in SKILL.md and references/context7-tools.md are Markdown examples, headings, or schema text. No evidence found that those Markdown lines execute code or implement cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are documentation and the flagged backticks are Markdown code formatting. I found no execution mechanism tied to those Markdown locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The stdio MCP transport starts the supplied command string through the shell, which is dangerous with untrusted command input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code uses subprocess.Popen with shell=True and a command string. This is a known command injection hazard when the command source is not tightly controlled.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network package execution through npx
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill workflow runs @upstash/context7-mcp through npx, creating a supply-chain and network execution dependency.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The npx execution path is direct and repeated in the bundled scripts. The command target is fixed, so the risk is dependency execution rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Developers often need current API examples without loading full documentation pages. This skill uses Context7 and local filters to return focused examples, signatures, and notes.
actual_capabilities: Resolves common library names to Context7-compatible library IDs.
Fetches documentation for a selected library, topic, mode, and page.
Filters raw documentation into code examples, API signatures, and important notes.
Supports code-focused and concept-focused documentation modes.
Provides helper scripts for starting, stopping, and verifying Context7 access.
Includes a reference file describing Context7 MCP tool parameters and usage patterns.
limitations: Requires Python, Bash, npx, and network access to Context7 dependencies.
Only covers libraries available through Context7 documentation sources.
Shell filters can omit useful context from long or unusual documentation pages.
It is not designed for local repository analysis or private source code exploration.

use_cases.title: Find API syntax during implementation
use_cases.description: Fetch concise examples and signatures for a framework feature while editing code.

use_cases.title: Compare library usage patterns
use_cases.description: Retrieve focused documentation pages for several libraries before choosing an approach.

use_cases.title: Prepare learning notes from official docs
use_cases.description: Collect short examples and key notes for a new package or framework topic.

prompt_templates.title: Fetch one example
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner lookup for a single topic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to fetch React documentation for useState. Return the clearest example and the key API note.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch Next.js routing documentation in info mode. Summarize the routing model and include one practical example.

prompt_templates.title: Use a known library ID
prompt_templates.scenario: Faster lookup when the Context7 ID is known.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use /prisma/docs to fetch code-mode documentation for queries. Include signatures, examples, and important notes.

prompt_templates.title: Research multiple pages
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced lookup when the first page is incomplete.
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch React hooks documentation pages 1 and 2. Compare the examples and identify the most relevant patterns for a production component.

output_examples.output: A concise response with selected useState examples, related signature notes, and warnings about state updates.
A short explanation of routing concepts, one or two examples, and important notes from the fetched documentation.

output_examples.input: Find Prisma query syntax for filtering records.
output_examples.output: Focused Prisma query examples, likely method signatures, and notes about filtering behavior.

best_practices: Provide a specific library and topic to reduce irrelevant results.
Use a known Context7 library ID when accuracy matters.
Review the filtered output against official docs before making high-risk changes.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to inspect private repositories or local project files.
Do not pass untrusted command strings to the generic MCP stdio client.
Do not assume filtered snippets include every required setup step.

faq.question: What does this skill fetch?
faq.answer: It fetches published library documentation through Context7 and returns focused examples, signatures, and notes.

faq.question: Does it read my local repository?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for published package documentation, not local source code analysis.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why does it use scripts?
faq.answer: The scripts fetch raw documentation and filter it locally before the assistant receives the shorter result.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses Context7 MCP through npx and may call HTTP MCP servers if configured.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It has medium operational risk from external command and network execution, but no confirmed malicious intent was found.
